data.title: Is Open Text’s 39% Rally in 2025 Just the Beginning?
data.description:
Thinking about what to do with your Open Text shares, or maybe considering a new investment? You are definitely not alone. Open Text stock has not just recovered from its past slumps, it is making headlines with a 38.6% gain so far this year and a solid 19.3% return over the past twelve months. Just this past week, the share price moved higher by 1.3%. If you look back over the last three years, the stock is up an impressive 58%. That is the kind of performance that attracts attention, especially as tech market optimism has returned across software and cloud companies.

Of course, price moves tell only part of the story. What is really getting attention right now is Open Text’s valuation. It is currently undervalued in five out of six key fundamental checks, earning the company a strong value score of 5. That stands out in a tech environment where many growth names appear expensive and riskier. But is all that upside already priced in, or is there more room for growth? Let’s examine how Open Text measures up across the major valuation methods, and then explore an alternative way to look at valuation that could help inform your decision.

Approach 1: Open Text Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates the fair value of a company by projecting its future free cash flows and discounting them back to today, reflecting the time value of money. This method helps assess whether the current market price reflects the company’s actual cash-generating potential.

For Open Text, the latest reported Free Cash Flow is $687.7 Million. Analysts provide projections for the next several years, with Free Cash Flow expected to reach $1.03 Billion by 2027. Longer-term forecasts, extrapolated from analyst trends, suggest Free Cash Flow could reach almost $1.5 Billion by 2035. All cash flows are evaluated in U.S. Dollars.

Using these cash flow estimates, the DCF model arrives at an intrinsic value of $66.64 per share. With the current share price trading at a 41.3% discount to this estimated fair value, the DCF approach strongly suggests that Open Text stock is significantly undervalued at the moment.

Approach 2: Open Text Price vs Earnings (PE)

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is especially useful for valuing established, profitable companies like Open Text, as it relates the stock price to actual reported earnings. Investors often look to the PE ratio to gauge how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings, making it a practical tool for companies with steady profits.

Market expectations for growth and risk play a key role in shaping what is considered a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio. Fast-growing, lower-risk companies tend to justify higher PE ratios, while companies facing growth or operational challenges might trade at lower multiples. That is why it is important to look beyond the headline number and understand the wider context.

Open Text currently trades on a PE ratio of 22.4x, which is much lower than the average for its software industry peers at 39.5x and the broader industry average of 34.9x. Peer group comparisons can be helpful, but they do not always account for the unique characteristics of each company.

This is where Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio comes in. Unlike blanket peer or industry averages, the Fair Ratio is designed to reflect a more personalized, data-driven context for Open Text’s valuation.

Comparing Open Text’s actual PE of 22.4x to its Fair Ratio of 36.5x, the shares appear to be significantly undervalued on this basis, supporting the findings from the DCF approach.

data.title: Aztech Global (SGX:8AZ) Margin Decline Challenges Bullish Earnings Growth Narrative
data.description:
Aztech Global (SGX:8AZ) posted a net profit margin of 9.2%, down from 11.3% last year, pointing to tightening profitability even as its earnings are projected to grow by 7.1% annually, slightly ahead of the Singapore market’s 6.6% forecast. Revenue is expected to grow at a modest 1.4% per year, lagging behind the market average of 3.8%. Shares currently trade at SGD 0.66, below the estimated fair value of SGD 0.87, and at a notably lower Price-to-Earnings Ratio of 13.7x compared to peers and the sector. Investors may see a compelling story on value and profit growth here, but with a watchful eye on sustainability of margins and dividends.

See our full analysis for Aztech Global.

Next, we will go beyond the numbers to see how Aztech’s results compare to the most widely discussed narratives in the investment community. Some expectations will ring true, while others could be up for debate.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Profit Margins Narrow Despite High-Quality Earnings

  • Net profit margin fell to 9.2%, down from 11.3% last year, which undercuts the strong “high quality earnings” label and points to increased pressure on Aztech’s operational efficiency even as earnings are set to grow at a pace faster than the market average.
  • Bulls highlight the company’s projected 7.1% annual earnings growth, which
    • outpaces the Singapore market’s 6.6% and should, in theory, signal lasting profitability,
    • but what is surprising is that this acceleration is occurring even as margins compress, challenging the bullish viewpoint that margin strength will always accompany growth.

Dividend Sustainability Comes Into Focus

  • There is a present concern regarding ongoing dividend strength, as flagged by risk statements, with margin compression making future payouts less certain despite the company’s recent profitable performance.
  • Bears argue that the combination of slower revenue growth (1.4% per year, below the market’s 3.8%) and tightening margins leaves little room for generous or reliable dividends,
    • yet it is notable that earnings growth guidance still exceeds the market’s, softening the case for immediate dividend jeopardy,
    • which means that while the risk exists, it has yet to be fully realized in reported results.

Valuation Discount Signals Potential Upside

  • Aztech trades at a Price-to-Earnings Ratio of 13.7x, meaningfully below the peer average of 18.4x and sector’s 27.3x, and its SGD 0.66 share price stands at a 25% discount to the DCF fair value of SGD 0.87.
  • The relatively low valuation multiples heavily support the case that the company’s profit growth is not yet fully priced in,
    • especially given the sector’s higher average multiples and Aztech’s clean earnings quality,
    • suggesting a possible rerating if the projected growth and margin trends stabilize in the coming periods.

data.title: What Recent Stock Swings Mean for S&P Global’s Market Value in 2025
data.description:
Thinking about what to do with S&P Global stock right now? You are definitely not alone. Whether you are weighing a hold, a buy, or just curious, S&P Global remains one of those companies that always seems to be at the heart of market conversations. The company’s recent stock swings say a lot about shifting investor sentiment. Over the past week, the shares have nudged 0.1% higher, but that comes after a bumpy month where the price dropped 11.2%. For the year so far, S&P Global is down 2.5%, and if you zoom out, it is off 8.1% over the last 12 months. Yet, anyone with a long memory will notice that over three years, the stock is up an impressive 66.8%, and it has delivered a solid 49% gain over the last five years.

Some of these shifts reflect broader market moods, especially how investors view risk and long-term growth for finance and data companies. With so many moving parts, the stock’s current valuation is a hot topic. According to our checklist, S&P Global is undervalued in just 1 out of 6 major valuation metrics, earning itself a value score of 1. In the next section, I will break down what that really means, walking through the main ways analysts value a stock like S&P Global, and why the best measure of true value might actually come at the end of this article.

S&P Global scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: S&P Global Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model provides a different lens on stock valuation. Instead of only looking at profits or dividends, this approach measures how much value a company creates above the minimum return investors demand, known as the cost of equity. Put simply, it examines whether investing in S&P Global’s business generates profits that beat the company’s own cost for using investor funds.

For S&P Global, the numbers are telling. The estimated Book Value stands at $109.37 per share, while analysts forecast a stable Earnings Per Share (EPS) of $18.38, based on a range of future estimates. That compares to a Cost of Equity of $9.30 per share, which means each share is expected to generate an "Excess Return" of $9.07. The firm’s average Return on Equity comes in at a strong 16.33%, and the Stable Book Value is projected to climb to $112.55 per share over time.

Pulling these figures together, the model estimates S&P Global’s intrinsic value per share at $287.43. Compared to the current market price, the discount implied is -68%, indicating the stock is significantly overvalued using this approach.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for S&P Global.

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests S&P Global may be overvalued by 68.0%. Find undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Approach 2: S&P Global Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a widely used tool for valuing profitable companies like S&P Global because it tells us how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. This makes it especially relevant for mature businesses with steady profits.

What the market considers a "normal" or “fair” PE ratio depends a lot on the company’s future earnings potential and the perceived risks. Companies with stronger growth prospects or lower risks often command higher PE ratios, while lower growth or riskier businesses tend to see lower multiples.

S&P Global currently trades at a PE ratio of 36.8x, placing it well above the Capital Markets industry average of 25.7x and its immediate peer group average of 31.6x. Simply Wall St’s proprietary “Fair Ratio” for S&P Global comes in at 20.2x. This figure is based on a detailed assessment of the company’s earnings growth, profit margins, market cap and risks specific to its industry.

The Fair Ratio offers a more complete picture than just using straight industry or peer averages because it adapts to the company’s unique traits and risk profile. Comparing S&P Global’s current PE to its Fair Ratio suggests the stock is quite a bit more expensive than its fundamentals alone might justify.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.title: Is FedEx Stock Poised for Growth After Recent Earnings Rebound?
data.description:
If you’ve been eyeing FedEx stock and wondering if now is the time to make a move, you’re definitely not alone. With shares closing recently at $235.8, investors find themselves in the classic tug-of-war between value and momentum. Over the past week, the stock slipped by 1.7%, not a huge swing but just enough to make some folks cautious. In the past month, though, FedEx rebounded with a solid 4.5% gain, hinting that confidence in its long-term story might be reviving. Year to date, the stock is down 14.0%, and if we stretch out to a full year, it’s still off by 11.2%. But glance back three years and FedEx actually comes out well ahead, up an impressive 64.7%. This suggests resilience and underlying strength in the business even during volatile times.

Recent market chatter has highlighted shifting consumer behaviors and continued supply chain adaptation, both areas where FedEx naturally plays a lead role. These larger trends have caused some turbulence in shipping stocks, with adjustments in risk perception rippling through the sector. Another point of interest for investors is that FedEx comes out with a valuation score of 5 out of 6 on our undervaluation checklist. This means it passes nearly every test savvy investors look for when hunting for bargains in the market.

So, how exactly do we arrive at this score? Let’s dig into the valuation methods most investors rely on, and stick around until the end because I’ll share an even more insightful angle on understanding what FedEx might really be worth.

FedEx delivered -11.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Logistics industry.

Approach 1: FedEx Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model helps estimate the true worth of a company by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s dollars. Essentially, it answers the question, “How much are FedEx’s expected future earnings worth right now?”

FedEx’s current Free Cash Flow sits at approximately $2.63 billion. Analyst forecasts stretch out over the next several years, with cash flows expected to rise steadily and reach about $7.1 billion by 2035. While detailed analyst estimates are available through 2028, projections beyond that are based on growth trends and industry estimates. All values are calculated in US dollars.

When running these numbers through the two-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity DCF model, FedEx’s intrinsic value comes out to $386.67 per share. This is 39.0% above its recent share price of $235.8, signaling the market might be significantly underestimating FedEx’s underlying cash-generating power.

In summary, the DCF approach suggests FedEx stock is trading well below its true value, offering a compelling case for investors looking for undervalued opportunities.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests FedEx is undervalued by 39.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: FedEx Price vs Earnings (PE Ratio)

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a go-to valuation approach for companies like FedEx that are consistently profitable. This measure helps investors see how much they are paying for every dollar of the company's earnings, which is a crucial gauge when earnings are robust and recurring.

What counts as a “fair” PE can depend on growth prospects and perceived risks. Companies expected to deliver strong future growth can justify higher PE ratios, while those with slower growth or higher risks usually trade at lower multiples.

FedEx currently trades at a PE ratio of 13.5x. This is below both the industry average of 15.8x and the peer group average of 17.9x, indicating FedEx shares are priced more conservatively than its typical competitors.

To add another layer, Simply Wall St's "Fair Ratio" calculates what a truly appropriate multiple would be for FedEx. This proprietary metric weighs not just industry standards and peer groups, but also considers FedEx’s specific profit margins, growth outlook, size, and risk profile. Because of these tailored inputs, the Fair Ratio offers a more refined valuation benchmark than raw peer or industry averages alone.

For FedEx, the Fair Ratio stands at 17.7x, which is noticeably higher than its actual PE ratio of 13.5x. This gap suggests the market is assigning a lower value to FedEx’s earnings than what might be justified by its fundamentals.

Result: UNDERVALUED

With shares trading at 5,500 yen and profitability steadily improving, investors are focused on the company’s ongoing outperformance in both sales and profit margins, though there is some caution around minor share price volatility.

data.description:

The Last 12 Months Of Insider Transactions At 5G Networks

Notably, that recent sale by MD & Director Joseph Demase was not the only time they sold 5G Networks shares this year. Earlier in the year, they fetched AU$0.15 per share in a -AU$8.2m sale. So what is clear is that an insider saw fit to sell at around the current price of AU$0.14. We generally don't like to see insider selling, but the lower the sale price, the more it concerns us. Insider Ownership

I like to look at how many shares insiders own in a company, to help inform my view of how aligned they are with insiders.
